narrative:
First Warn Forecast
Wednesday, June 10, 2026
Headlines
Hot and humid conditions continue through Thursday, with afternoon heat index values approaching 100 degrees.
Strong to severe thunderstorms are expected this evening, with damaging winds and large hail posing the greatest threat.
Another round of severe thunderstorms is expected Thursday afternoon and evening, with damaging winds, large hail, and tornadoes all possible.

Summer-like heat remains firmly in place across Missouri and Illinois today as temperatures climb into the lower and middle 90s. While slightly lower humidity levels may prevent widespread heat advisory criteria from being reached, it will still feel uncomfortable outdoors with heat index values near 100 degrees during the afternoon. Residents are encouraged to stay hydrated and limit prolonged outdoor activity during the hottest part of the day.

Attention quickly turns to a developing severe weather threat later today and tonight. A disturbance moving through the Midwest will interact with a very unstable atmosphere, allowing thunderstorms to develop across northern Missouri and Iowa before spreading southeastward into the region this evening. The atmosphere will contain ample instability and enough wind shear to support severe thunderstorms.

The greatest threat this evening will be across northeast Missouri and west-central Illinois, generally along and north of Highway 36 and Interstate 72. Storms are expected to organize into clusters capable of producing damaging wind gusts and large hail. While the tornado threat appears lower than the wind and hail risk, an isolated tornado cannot be ruled out, especially within any stronger rotating storms or embedded circulations that develop within storm clusters.

After a relative lull Thursday morning, attention will shift to a cold front moving into the region during the afternoon and evening. Once again, the atmosphere is expected to become highly unstable ahead of the front, providing favorable conditions for another round of strong to severe thunderstorms.

Unlike tonight's event, Thursday's severe weather threat will extend across a larger portion of the region. Storms are expected to develop along the advancing cold front and organize into clusters or a line of thunderstorms. Damaging straight-line winds appear to be the primary threat, though large hail and a few tornadoes will also be possible. The strongest storms are expected during the afternoon and evening hours before gradually weakening later Thursday night as the cold front moves east of the area.

The good news is that this front will bring an end to the recent stretch of heat and humidity. Temperatures will return to more seasonable levels by Friday and continue through the upcoming weekend. While additional chances for showers and thunderstorms remain in the forecast at times, the overall weather pattern will be considerably more comfortable than what we've experienced this week.

Now is the time to review severe weather safety plans and ensure you have multiple ways to receive warnings, especially tonight and again Thursday as severe weather threatens portions of Missouri and Illinois.

First. Accurate. Trusted.
